question: [File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as callback receiver under the precondition that liquidate() transfers collateral to receiver BEFORE invoking the callback and BEFORE pulling repayment trigger the call sequence liquidate(borrower, seizedAssets>0, repaidUnits=0, callback=attacker) -> attacker.onLiquidate() reenters liquidate(same borrower, seizedAssets>0, repaidUnits=0) a second time before the outer repayment safeTransferFrom executes, violating the invariant that collateral seizure must be matched by a corresponding repayment in the same atomic operation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure (double-seize) with a single repayment? Proof idea: deploy a malicious ILiquidateCallback that reenters liquidate() on the same borrower in onLiquidate(); assert position.collateral[collateralIndex] after both calls is less than what a single liquidation would leave, and that repaidUnits paid equals only one liquidation's worth.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator under the precondition that LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT transient storage is only written by take() (not by liquidate() itself) trigger the call sequence liquidate(borrower, callback=attacker) -> attacker.onLiquidate() reenters liquidate(same borrower) again, violating the invariant that a single liquidation event must not allow the same borrower's collateral to be seized twice in one transaction without the liquidationLocked guard blocking the reentrant call, causing scoped impact: unauthorized double collateral seizure? Proof idea: stateful fuzz test asserting liquidationLocked(id, borrower) is false at the start of onLiquidate() and that a reentrant liquidate() call succeeds.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: repay] Can an unprivileged actor authorized for onBehalf under the precondition that repay() reduces position[id][onBehalf].debt BEFORE invoking the IRepayCallback and BEFORE pulling tokens trigger the call sequence repay(market, units, onBehalf, callback=attacker, data) -> attacker.onRepay() calls withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, maxAssets, onBehalf, attacker) while debt is already reduced, violating the invariant that collateral withdrawal must only be permitted after the corresponding debt repayment tokens have been received by the protocol, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral withdrawal without actual token payment? Proof idea: deploy a malicious IRepayCallback that calls withdrawCollateral in onRepay(); assert that collateral is withdrawn and loan tokens are never transferred to Midnight.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that payer = buyerCallback != address(0) ? buyerCallback : (offer.buy ? buyer : msg.sender) and that the buyerCallback is the taker-supplied takerCallback when offer.buy==false trigger the call sequence take(offer{buy:false}, takerCallback=maliciousContract) where maliciousContract.onBuy() returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS without transferring any tokens, violating the invariant that buyerAssets must be received by the protocol before credit is granted to the buyer, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing (debt increase) without payment of buyerAssets - sellerAssets settlement fee? Proof idea: deploy a malicious IBuyCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS without transferring tokens; assert Midnight's loanToken balance did not increase by buyerAssets - sellerAssets.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] is checked at take() call time (not at offer creation time) and that an authorized operator of the maker can call setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, maker) trigger the call sequence: attacker is authorized by victim maker -> attacker calls setIsAuthorized(attacker_controlled_ratifier, true, maker) -> attacker constructs an offer with offer.maker=victim, offer.ratifier=attacker_controlled_ratifier -> attacker calls take() with that offer, violating the invariant that only the maker's intended ratifier should validate their offers, causing scoped impact: unauthorized filling of offers the maker never intended to authorize? Proof idea: unit test showing that an authorized operator can install a permissive ratifier and fill arbitrary offers attributed to the maker.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take] Can an unprivileged authorized operator under the precondition that setIsAuthorized has no scope restriction (not scoped by market, action, or function) and that authorized accounts can authorize other accounts trigger the call sequence: victim authorizes operator for a limited purpose -> operator calls setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) -> attacker calls withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker), violating the invariant that authorization delegation must not allow transitive escalation to drain victim funds, causing scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal of victim's credit? Proof idea: fuzz test showing that any authorized address can grant further authorizations and that the chain victim->op->attacker allows attacker to call withdraw() on victim's behalf.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged actor under the precondition that EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified checks isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer) at ratification time and that authorization is coarse-grained trigger the call sequence: maker authorizes signer for a different purpose -> signer signs a Merkle tree containing an offer with unfavorable terms for the maker -> taker calls take() with that offer and a valid Merkle proof, violating the invariant that the maker's signature must bind the exact offer terms they intended, causing scoped impact: unauthorized filling of offers under terms the maker did not intend? Proof idea: unit test where an authorized signer signs a tree containing a sell offer at a very low tick (high interest rate) and a taker fills it.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take / SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged authorized operator under the precondition that SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified checks isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender) and that authorization is coarse-grained trigger the call sequence: victim maker authorizes operator -> operator calls SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) -> operator constructs offers in maliciousRoot with maker=victim -> taker fills those offers, violating the invariant that only the maker should be able to ratify offer trees on their behalf, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing or credit creation attributed to the victim maker? Proof idea: unit test showing operator can ratify a root containing a sell offer (debt-creating) for the maker and a taker fills it.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the enterGate check for sellerDebtIncrease only fires when sellerDebtIncrease > 0 and that sellerDebtIncrease = units - sellerCreditDecrease trigger the call sequence: seller has existing credit >= units -> taker calls take(offer{buy:false}) with units <= seller.credit -> sellerDebtIncrease == 0 so enterGate.canIncreaseDebt is never called, violating the invariant that the enter gate must be consulted for all debt-related position changes even when credit netting occurs, causing scoped impact: gate bypass allowing a gated seller to reduce credit without gate approval? Proof idea: deploy an enterGate that always returns false for canIncreaseDebt; show that a sell take that nets against existing credit bypasses the gate entirely.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the enterGate check for buyerCreditIncrease only fires when buyerCreditIncrease > 0 and that buyerCreditIncrease = zeroFloorSub(units, buyerPos.debt) trigger the call sequence: buyer has existing debt >= units -> taker calls take(offer{buy:true}) with units <= buyer.debt -> buyerCreditIncrease == 0 so enterGate.canIncreaseCredit is never called, violating the invariant that the enter gate must be consulted for all credit-related position changes even when debt netting occurs, causing scoped impact: gate bypass allowing a gated buyer to reduce debt without gate approval? Proof idea: deploy an enterGate that always returns false for canIncreaseCredit; show that a buy take that nets against existing debt bypasses the gate entirely.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged actor under the precondition that liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(msg.sender) is checked but the liquidate() function allows callback=attacker where attacker is a different address from msg.sender trigger the call sequence: attacker deploys a contract that is approved by liquidatorGate -> attacker calls liquidate(borrower, callback=attacker) where msg.sender passes the gate -> attacker.onLiquidate() is called with seized collateral already transferred to receiver, violating the invariant that the liquidator gate must restrict who benefits from liquidation not just who initiates it, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure by a party not approved by the liquidator gate? Proof idea: unit test where liquidatorGate approves msg.sender but receiver is an unapproved third party; assert collateral reaches the unapproved receiver.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the liquidation lock is set via tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) using transient storage and that transient storage is cleared at the end of each transaction trigger the call sequence: within a single transaction, attacker calls take() on seller -> during sellerCallback, attacker calls liquidate(seller) which should be blocked by liquidationLocked -> verify the lock is actually set, violating the invariant that the seller cannot be liquidated during take callbacks, causing scoped impact: unauthorized liquidation of a seller mid-take leading to double-accounting of debt? Proof idea: unit test asserting liquidationLocked(id, seller) returns true inside onSell() callback and that a reentrant liquidate() call reverts with NotLiquidatable.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that wasLocked = tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) returns the previous value and that if wasLocked==true the lock is NOT cleared at the end of take() trigger the call sequence: attacker is already inside a take() callback for seller -> attacker calls take() again for the same seller -> wasLocked=true so the outer take does not clear the lock -> after the inner take completes, the lock remains true -> attacker calls liquidate(seller) which is blocked, violating the invariant that the liquidation lock must be correctly restored to its pre-call state after nested takes, causing scoped impact: permanent liquidation lock on seller (fund freeze)? Proof idea: unit test with nested take() calls for the same seller; assert liquidationLocked(id, seller) is false after all calls complete.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the seller health check require(liquidationLocked(id, seller) || isHealthy(offer.market, id, seller)) occurs AFTER both callbacks and that the sell callback can supply additional collateral trigger the call sequence: taker takes a sell offer creating debt that makes seller unhealthy -> during onSell() callback, seller supplies collateral to pass the health check -> health check passes, violating the invariant that a seller must be healthy based on pre-existing collateral before new debt is created (not collateral sourced during the callback), causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing against collateral that is only available during the callback window? Proof idea: unit test where seller has zero collateral pre-take, onSell() supplies collateral, and health check passes; assert this constitutes a flash-borrow.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the seller health check is skipped when liquidationLocked(id, seller) is true and that a nested take() sets wasLocked=true (not clearing the lock) trigger the call sequence: outer take() sets lock for seller -> inner take() for same seller inside callback sets wasLocked=true -> inner take() does NOT clear lock at end -> inner take() skips health check because liquidationLocked==true -> seller accumulates debt without health check, violating the invariant that every debt-increasing take must pass a health check, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing beyond collateral capacity? Proof idea: fuzz test with nested takes for the same seller; assert isHealthy(seller) after all takes complete.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: multicall] Can an unprivileged actor under the precondition that multicall uses delegatecall (preserving msg.sender) and that callbacks during take() can call back into Midnight trigger the call sequence: attacker encodes [take(offer1), take(offer2)] in multicall where offer1's callback calls back into Midnight to perform a state-changing operation that affects offer2's execution, violating the invariant that multicall must not allow cross-call state corruption that would be impossible in sequential single calls, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt reduction or credit creation through state manipulation across multicall steps? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting that executing take(offer1) then take(offer2) sequentially produces the same final state as multicall([take(offer1), take(offer2)]).

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] is updated BEFORE callbacks and that an authorized operator of the maker can call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) trigger the call sequence: taker initiates take() -> consumed is incremented -> during buyerCallback or sellerCallback, attacker (authorized for maker) calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel the group -> the take() completes successfully with the offer filled, violating the invariant that a cancelled offer group must not be fillable, causing scoped impact: offer filled after cancellation (offer replay/reuse)? Proof idea: unit test where an authorized operator cancels the group inside a callback; assert the take still completes and consumed is set to max.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] is updated BEFORE callbacks and that the callback can call setConsumed(group, newAmount, maker) where newAmount < current consumed trigger the call sequence: taker fills offer partially -> consumed is updated to X -> during callback, attacker calls setConsumed(group, X-1, maker) which reverts with AlreadyConsumed() -> but attacker calls setConsumed(group, X, maker) which succeeds (no-op) -> then calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel, violating the invariant that partial fills must permanently consume offer capacity, causing scoped impact: offer overfill through consumed state manipulation? Proof idea: fuzz test asserting consumed[maker][group] after take() equals the pre-callback value plus the fill amount.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified uses block.chainid for the domain separator (not INITIAL_CHAIN_ID) while market IDs use INITIAL_CHAIN_ID trigger the call sequence: on a hard-forked chain where block.chainid != INITIAL_CHAIN_ID, attacker obtains a signature valid on the original chain -> the domain separator has changed so the signature is invalid on the fork -> but the market still exists (same INITIAL_CHAIN_ID-based ID) -> attacker cannot replay the signature, violating the invariant that offer signatures must remain valid across the same market's lifetime, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (valid offers become unfillable after a hard fork)? Proof idea: differential test comparing EcrecoverRatifier behavior with block.chainid=1 vs block.chainid=2 for the same market.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged authorized operator under the precondition that cancelRoot checks isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender) and authorization is coarse-grained trigger the call sequence: victim maker authorizes operator for a limited purpose -> operator calls cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel all offers in the maker's active Merkle tree, violating the invariant that only the maker should be able to cancel their own offer roots, causing scoped impact: unauthorized cancellation of maker's offers (fund freeze / DoS of maker's liquidity provision)? Proof idea: unit test where an authorized operator cancels a root; assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] is true and subsequent take() reverts with RootCanceled.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that offer.maker != taker is checked but offer.maker != msg.sender is NOT checked trigger the call sequence: msg.sender == offer.maker, taker is a different address that has authorized msg.sender -> msg.sender calls take(offer{maker: msg.sender}, taker=victim) where victim has authorized msg.sender -> the self-take check passes because offer.maker != taker -> the take executes with msg.sender as both the maker and the caller, violating the invariant that a maker must not be able to fill their own offer to manipulate consumed state or extract value, causing scoped impact: unauthorized self-take enabling consumed state manipulation or fee extraction? Proof idea: unit test where msg.sender == offer.maker and taker is an authorized third party; assert the take succeeds and check for accounting anomalies.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that _updatePosition is only called for the buyer if hasCredit(id, buyer) || units > buyerPos.debt and that if neither condition holds the buyer's position is NOT updated before the take trigger the call sequence: buyer has no credit and units <= buyerPos.debt -> _updatePosition is skipped for buyer -> buyer's lastLossFactor and lastAccrual are stale -> take() reduces buyer's debt without applying pending loss factor slashing, violating the invariant that every position interaction must apply accumulated loss factor before modifying credit or debt, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt reduction without applying proportional bad-debt losses (buyer recovers more debt reduction than entitled)? Proof idea: unit test where market has lossFactor > 0 and buyer has debt but no credit; assert that after take(), buyer's effective debt reduction accounts for the loss factor.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the seller's position is updated via _updatePosition only if hasCredit(id, seller) is true and that a seller with only debt (no credit) does NOT get their position updated trigger the call sequence: seller has debt but no credit -> hasCredit(id, seller) is false -> _updatePosition is skipped for seller -> seller's lastLossFactor is stale -> take() increases seller's debt without applying accumulated loss factor, violating the invariant that every debt-increasing operation must apply the current loss factor to the position first, causing scoped impact: unauthorized borrowing beyond the seller's true debt capacity after bad-debt socialization? Proof idea: fuzz test with nonzero lossFactor; assert that a seller with only debt gets their position updated before debt is increased.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged authorized operator under the precondition that withdrawCollateral checks isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender] and that authorization is coarse-grained and not scoped by market trigger the call sequence: victim authorizes operator for market A -> operator calls withdrawCollateral(marketB, collateralIndex, assets, victim, attacker) for a different market B where victim has collateral, violating the invariant that authorization must be scoped to prevent cross-market collateral theft, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral withdrawal from a market the victim did not intend to authorize? Proof idea: unit test where victim authorizes operator only intending market A access; assert operator can drain collateral from market B.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged actor under the precondition that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized checks isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer) at execution time and that an already-authorized operator can sign authorization messages trigger the call sequence: victim authorizes operator -> operator signs an Authorization{authorizer: victim, authorized: attacker, isAuthorized: true, nonce: N, deadline: far_future} -> anyone calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with this signed authorization -> attacker gains full authorization over victim's Midnight state, violating the invariant that gasless authorization must only be executable by the authorizer themselves or with their explicit per-action consent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized full control over victim's positions? Proof idea: unit test showing an authorized operator's signature is accepted by EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant a third party authorization.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as callback receiver under the precondition that liquidate() transfers collateral to receiver BEFORE invoking the callback and BEFORE pulling repayment trigger the call sequence liquidate(borrower, seizedAssets>0, repaidUnits=0, callback=attacker) -> attacker.onLiquidate() reenters liquidate(same borrower, seizedAssets>0, repaidUnits=0) a second time before the outer repayment safeTransferFrom executes, violating the invariant that collateral seizure must be matched by a corresponding repayment in the same atomic operation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure (double-seize) with a single repayment? [File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged actor under the precondition that liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(msg.sender) is checked but the liquidate() function allows callback=attacker where attacker is a different address from msg.sender trigger the call sequence: attacker deploys a contract that is approved by liquidatorGate -> attacker calls liquidate(borrower, callback=attacker) where msg.sender passes the gate -> attacker.onLiquidate() is called with seized collateral already transferred to receiver, violating the invariant that the liquidator gate must restrict who benefits from liquidation not just who initiates it, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure by a party not approved by the liquidator gate?
